1: revelation of God Lord, you have been our dwelling place in all generations. God as dwelling place — fulfilled in Christ who is Immanuel, God-with-us (Matthew 1:23). John's Prologue declares the ultimate dwelling: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14). The dwelling place that sheltered every generation is now made flesh; in Christ, God becomes the home his people have always needed (John 14:2-3).
2: revelation of God Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting you are God. The eternal God — Christ's own claim: before Abraham was, I am (John 8:58). Hebrews opens with this eternity: you, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands (Hebrews 1:10). The everlasting God is the same Jesus Christ who is the same yesterday and today and forever (Hebrews 13:8).
3: shadow You return man to dust and say, Return, O children of man! God returning man to dust — and Christ reversing the return. To dust you shall return (Genesis 3:19) is the consequence Christ absorbs: he is laid in the earth (John 19:41-42) and returns from it in resurrection. His Come forth! to Lazarus (John 11:43) is the divine call that reverses the return to dust.
4: shadow For a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday when it is past, or as a watch in the night. Divine timelessness applied to eschatological expectation: with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day (2 Peter 3:8). Peter uses this verse to explain why Christ's coming seems delayed — from God's perspective, no time has passed. The eternal Son inhabits this timelessness; the delay of the parousia is patience, not failure.
5: shadow You sweep them away as with a flood; they are like a dream, like grass that is renewed in the morning: Human transience like grass — the Incarnation enters this transience. Christ becomes the mortal, grass-like human being (Isaiah 40:6-8 — all flesh is grass) and transforms it from within by resurrection. The flood that sweeps away is the very flood he walks through (baptism into death) and rises from.
6: shadow in the morning it flourishes and is renewed; in the evening it fades and withers. The grass that flourishes and withers — the pattern of death and resurrection. Christ enters the evening of human existence (Good Friday) and becomes the one who flourishes in the morning of the resurrection (Easter Sunday — early on the first day of the week, John 20:1). His resurrection transforms the withering pattern.
7: shadow For we are brought to an end by your anger; by your wrath we are dismayed. God's wrath bringing to an end — the judgment Christ absorbs. The wrath that dismays humanity falls on Christ at the cross: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us (Galatians 3:13). He is brought to an end by the wrath that humanity deserved, so that those in him face no condemning wrath (Romans 8:1).
8: shadow You have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your countenance. Iniquities set before the divine face — the transparency that the cross reveals. God's omniscient knowledge of sin is the foundation of the atonement: him who knew no sin he made to be sin on our behalf (2 Corinthians 5:21). The secret sins set in God's light were set on Christ so they would not condemn those who believe.
9: shadow For all our days pass away under your wrath; we bring our years to an end like a sigh. Days passing under wrath — the universal human condition that Christ entered. He lived under the weight of human mortality and the divine wrath that attended it, made under the law to redeem those who were under the law (Galatians 4:4-5). His sighing on the cross (Mark 15:37) is the last sigh of the old order ending.
10: shadow The years of our life are seventy, or even by reason of strength eighty; yet their span is but toil and trouble; they are soon gone, and we fly away. The brevity of life — your life is a vapor (James 4:14). Christ entered the brevity of human life (born, living approximately 33 years, dying) and transformed it. I am the resurrection and the life (John 11:25) — he who is eternal life takes on the 70-year span so that those in him receive eternal years.
11: shadow Who considers the power of your anger, and your wrath according to the fear of you? The power of divine anger — which only Christ fully considered and bore. He who feared God perfectly (Hebrews 5:7 — heard because of his reverence) also bore the full weight of divine wrath at the cross. No human fully considers this power; the Son of God alone meets it and absorbs it in his own body.
12: shadow So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom. A heart of wisdom — found in Christ: Christ Jesus, who became to us wisdom from God (1 Corinthians 1:30). To number one's days is the posture Christ models: I must work the works of him who sent me while it is day; night is coming when no one can work (John 9:4). The urgency of numbered days becomes the church's mission-posture (Ephesians 5:15-16).
13: shadow Return, O LORD! How long? Have pity on your servants! The how long and return — the church's eschatological prayer: Come, Lord Jesus! (Revelation 22:20). Christ taught this urgency: the parable of the persistent widow who cries give me justice (Luke 18:1-8) is the church's cry for the returning Lord. The return is the parousia of the Son.
14: revelation of God Satisfy us in the morning with your steadfast love, that we may rejoice and be glad all our days. Morning satisfaction with steadfast love — the resurrection morning. Mary Magdalene at the empty tomb (John 20:1,16 — she turned and said Rabboni!) is the morning satisfaction that launches the church's rejoicing. The steadfast love (chesed) that satisfies is made flesh in Christ (John 1:14 — full of grace and truth).
15: shadow Make us glad for as many days as you have afflicted us, and for as many years as we have seen evil. Gladness proportionate to affliction — the eschatological reversal. Paul: for I consider that the sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory that is to be revealed to us (Romans 8:18). The years of affliction give way to eternal joy; the proportion favors the believer infinitely.
16: shadow Let your work be shown to your servants, and your glorious power to their children. God's work shown — the Incarnation is the supreme display of God's work and glory: we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth (John 1:14). The Spirit continues showing this work to each generation's children (John 16:13-15 — he will glorify me).
17: shadow Let the favor of the Lord our God be upon us, and establish the work of our hands upon us; yes, establish the work of our hands! The favor of the Lord — the Aaronic blessing fulfilled in Christ (Numbers 6:25; 2 Corinthians 4:6). The work of our hands established — the church's labor in Christ is not in vain: be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain (1 Corinthians 15:58).
